items.q: What gives carbon fibre composite its strength?
items.correct: The fibres provide tensile strength; the resin matrix holds them in place and transfers loads.
items.misconception: The carbon fibres alone - the resin is just glue.
items.why: Students dismiss the matrix as filler. In reality the matrix is essential: it transfers loads between fibres and protects them. Loose carbon fibres without matrix have very little structural strength.

items.correct: A drawing showing multiple 2D views (front, side, plan) of an object.
items.misconception: A 3D drawing showing the object at an angle.
items.random_wrong: A drawing in black ink only.
items.why: Students confuse orthographic (multi-view 2D) with isometric (single 3D view). Orthographic projects each face flat onto separate views - used for engineering specification.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.
It's Lengthwise. “Sideways” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Knitted. “Woven” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many construction techniques are listed: weaving, knitting, sewing?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
